name: Konohanasakuya-hime
description: Konohanasakuya-hime, also known as Sakuya-bime, is a prominent figure in Japanese mythology, revered as the goddess of Mount Fuji and all blossoming flowers, particularly cherry blossoms (sakura). Her name translates to "blossom-princess-shining-radiantly." She embodies delicate beauty, transient life, and the ephemeral nature of existence, mirroring the short-lived but breathtaking bloom of the cherry blossom. She is often associated with volcanoes, specifically Mount Fuji, and her fiery temperament is linked to the mountain's volcanic activity. Her stories emphasize themes of mortality, doubt, and the power of divine lineage. She is worshipped for safe childbirth, happy marriage, and protection against fire. Her shrines are often located near volcanoes and blooming cherry trees, reinforcing her multifaceted symbolism. Her appeal lies in her potent combination of gentleness and formidable power.
subtitle: Blossom-Princess-Shining-Radiantly

keyMyths.title: The Doubt of Ninigi
keyMyths.description: Konohanasakuya-hime married Ninigi-no-Mikoto, grandson of the sun goddess Amaterasu. After she became pregnant, Ninigi doubted the child's paternity, suspecting it was not his due to the brevity of their courtship. To prove her fidelity and divine lineage, Konohanasakuya-hime entered a tightly sealed hut and set it on fire, proclaiming that if the children were truly Ninigi's, they would be unharmed. The three children emerged unscathed, thus proving her innocence and their divine heritage. This myth highlights themes of doubt, faith, and the confirmation of divine lineage through trial by fire.
keyMyths.source: Aston, W. G. (1896). Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697.

keyMyths.title: The Choice of Immortality
keyMyths.description: Amaterasu offered Ninigi two sisters: Iwanaga-hime (Rock-Princess), representing eternal life, and Konohanasakuya-hime (Blossom-Princess), representing earthly beauty and prosperity. Ninigi chose Konohanasakuya-hime, rejecting immortality for the fleeting beauty of the blossom. This decision is said to be the reason why human life is short and mortal, like the cherry blossom. Had Ninigi chosen Iwanaga-hime, humans would have been granted eternal life.
keyMyths.source: Chamberlain, B. H. (1919). Kojiki: Records of Ancient Matters.

keyMyths.title: The Creation of Mount Fuji
keyMyths.description: While no direct account describes Konohanasakuya-hime creating Mount Fuji, she is intrinsically linked to the mountain. Some interpretations suggest her fiery nature is responsible for the volcanic activity that formed the mountain over millennia. She is considered its protector and embodiment, and her presence is felt in the mountain's majestic beauty and powerful energy. Shrines dedicated to her are located on and around the mountain, solidifying her connection to it.
keyMyths.source: Picken, S. D. B. (1994). Essentials of Shinto: An Analytical Guide to Principal Teachings.
